html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Fourteen — Part Two

The Book of Melakhim Aleph

MELAKHIM ALEPH

The First Book of Kings

Chapters 7 through 12

The Temple Furnishings — The Ark Installed — Shelomoh's Prayer — The Queen of Sheva — The Kingdom Divided

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

No complete DSS manuscript for Melakhim Aleph

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTERS 7 THROUGH 12 — THE TEMPLE COMPLETE AND THE KINGDOM DIVIDED

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 7 Shelomoh's Palace — The House of the Forest of Lebanon — Khiram the Craftsman — The Temple Furnishings

1 Shelomoh was building his own house thirteen years, and he finished his entire house.

2 He built the House of the Forest of Lebanon. Its length was a hundred cubits and its breadth fifty cubits and its height thirty cubits, and it was built on four rows of cedar pillars, with cedar beams on the pillars.

3 And it was covered with cedar above the chambers that were on the forty-five pillars, fifteen in each row.

4 There were window frames in three rows, and window opposite window in three tiers.

5 All the doorways and windows had square frames, and window was opposite window in three tiers.

6 And he made the Hall of Pillars — its length was fifty cubits, and its breadth thirty cubits. There was a porch in front with pillars, and a canopy in front of them.

7 And he made the Hall of the Throne where he was to pronounce judgment, even the Hall of Judgment. It was finished with cedar from floor to rafters.

8 His own house where he was to dwell, in the other court back of the hall, was of like workmanship. Shelomoh also made a house like this hall for Pharaoh's daughter whom he had taken in marriage.

9 All these were made of costly stones, cut according to measure, sawed with saws, back and front, even from the foundation to the coping, and from outside to the great court.

10 The foundation was of costly stones, huge stones, stones of eight cubits and stones of ten cubits.

11 And above were costly stones, cut according to measurement, and cedar.

12 The great court had three courses of dressed stone all around, and a course of cedar beams — so had the inner court of the house of YHWH and the vestibule of the house.

13 And King Shelomoh sent and brought Khiram from Tzor.

14 He was the son of a widow of the tribe of Naftali, and his father was a man of Tzor, a worker in bronze. And he was full of wisdom, understanding, and skill for making any work in bronze. He came to King Shelomoh and did all his work.

15 He cast two pillars of bronze. Eighteen cubits was the height of one pillar, and a line of twelve cubits measured its circumference. It was hollow, and its thickness was four fingers. So with the second pillar.

16 He also made two capitals of cast bronze to set on the tops of the pillars. The height of the one capital was five cubits, and the height of the other capital was five cubits.

17 There were lattices of checker work with wreaths of chain work for the capitals on the tops of the pillars, a lattice for the one capital and a lattice for the other capital.

18 Likewise he made pomegranates — in two rows around the one latticework, to cover the capital that was on the top of the pillar, and he did the same with the other capital.

19 Now the capitals that were on the tops of the pillars in the vestibule were of lily-work, four cubits.

20 The capitals were on the two pillars and also above the rounded projection which was beside the latticework. There were two hundred pomegranates in two rows all around, and so with the other capital.

21 He set up the pillars at the vestibule of the temple. He set up the right pillar and called its name Yakhin, and he set up the left pillar and called its name Bo'az.

22 And on the tops of the pillars was lily-work. Thus the work of the pillars was finished.

23 Then he made the sea of cast metal. It was round, ten cubits from brim to brim, and five cubits high, and a line of thirty cubits measured its circumference.

24 Under its brim were gourds, for ten cubits, compassing the sea all around. The gourds were in two rows, cast with it when it was cast.

25 It stood on twelve oxen, three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east. The sea was set on them, and all their rear parts were inward.

26 Its thickness was a handbreadth, and its brim was made like the brim of a cup, like the flower of a lily. It held two thousand baths.

27 He also made the ten stands of bronze. Each stand was four cubits long, four cubits wide, and three cubits high.

28 This was the construction of the stands: they had panels, and the panels were set in the frames,

29 And on the panels that were set in the frames were lions, oxen, and cherubim. On the frames, both above and below the lions and oxen, there were wreaths of beveled work.

30 Moreover, each stand had four bronze wheels and axles of bronze, and at the four corners were supports for a basin. The supports were cast with wreaths at the side of each.

31 Its opening was within a crown that projected upward one cubit. Its opening was round, as a pedestal is made, a cubit and a half deep. At its opening there were carvings, and its panels were square, not round.

32 And the four wheels were underneath the panels. The axles of the wheels were of one piece with the stands, and the height of a wheel was a cubit and a half.

33 The wheels were made like a chariot wheel — their axles, their rims, their spokes, and their hubs were all cast.

34 There were four supports at the four corners of each stand. The supports were of one piece with the stands.

35 And on the top of the stand there was a round band half a cubit high, and on the top of the stand its stays and its panels were of one piece with it.

36 And on the surfaces of its stays and on its panels, he carved cherubim, lions, and palm trees, according to the space of each, with wreaths all around.

37 After this manner he made the ten stands. All of them were cast alike, of the same measure and the same form.

38 And he made ten basins of bronze. Each basin held forty baths, each basin measured four cubits, and there was a basin for each of the ten stands.

39 And he set the stands, five on the south side of the house, and five on the north side of the house. And he set the sea on the southeast corner of the house.

40 Khiram also made the pots, the shovels, and the basins. So Khiram finished all the work that he did for King Shelomoh on the house of YHWH:

41 The two pillars, the two bowls of the capitals that were on the tops of the pillars, and the two latticeworks to cover the two bowls of the capitals that were on the tops of the pillars;

42 And the four hundred pomegranates for the two latticeworks, two rows of pomegranates for each latticework, to cover the two bowls of the capitals that were on the pillars;

43 The ten stands, and the ten basins on the stands;

44 And the one sea, and the twelve oxen underneath the sea;

45 And the pots, the shovels, and the basins. All these vessels in the house of YHWH, which Khiram made for King Shelomoh, were of burnished bronze.

46 In the plain of the Yarden the king cast them, in the clay ground between Sukkot and Tzarethan.

47 And Shelomoh left all the vessels unweighed, because there were so many of them — the weight of the bronze was not determined.

48 So Shelomoh made all the vessels that were in the house of YHWH: the golden altar, the golden table for the bread of the Presence,

49 The lampstands of pure gold, five on the south side and five on the north, before the inner sanctuary, with the flowers, the lamps, and the tongs of gold;

50 The cups, snuffers, basins, dishes for incense, and fire pans of pure gold, and the sockets of gold, for the doors of the innermost part of the house, the Most Holy Place, and for the doors of the nave of the temple.

51 Thus all the work that King Shelomoh did on the house of YHWH was finished. And Shelomoh brought in the things that Dawid his father had dedicated — the silver, the gold, and the vessels — and stored them in the treasuries of the house of YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 7 catalogs the bronze and gold furnishings of the temple — the two pillars Yakhin and Bo'az, the great sea on twelve oxen, the ten bronze stands, the ten basins, and the golden altar and lampstands. The completeness of the description reflects the covenant importance of the material appointments of YHWH's house.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 51 — Shelomoh brought in the things that Dawid his father had dedicated — the silver, gold, and vessels — and stored them in the treasuries of YHWH. The covenant generosity of Dawid toward the temple he was not permitted to build is honored by his son who builds it.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. The two pillars Yakhin and Bo'az — verse 21 — have been the subject of covenant scholarly discussion throughout the tradition. Yakhin — he establishes — and Bo'az — in him is strength — together may be read as a covenant declaration: YHWH establishes his strength. They stand at the threshold of the covenant house.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 21 — Yakhin and Bo'az — he establishes, in him is strength. The two pillars at the entrance of the temple are named with covenant declarations. Every person entering the house of YHWH passed between these two names. The architectural theology of the temple entrance declares the character of the Creator who dwells within: he establishes, and in him is strength. Verse 51 — Shelomoh brought in all the things that Dawid his father had dedicated. The temple is Shelomoh's construction but Dawid's covenant project. The things Dawid dedicated — gathered through a lifetime of covenant warfare and covenant worship — are stored in YHWH's house by the son who completed what the father began.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 8 The Ark Brought Into the Temple — The Cloud Fills the House — Shelomoh's Dedication Prayer

1 Then Shelomoh assembled the elders of Yisra'EL and all the heads of the tribes, the leaders of the fathers' houses of the people of Yisra'EL, before King Shelomoh in Yerushalayim, to bring up the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH out of the city of Dawid, which is Tziyon.

2 And all the men of Yisra'EL assembled to King Shelomoh at the feast in the month Etanim, which is the seventh month.

3 And all the elders of Yisra'EL came, and the priests took up the Ark.

4 And they brought up the Ark of YHWH, the tent of meeting, and all the holy vessels that were in the tent — the priests and the Levites brought them up.

5 And King Shelomoh and all the congregation of Yisra'EL, who had assembled before him, were with him before the Ark, sacrificing so many sheep and oxen that they could not be counted or numbered.

6 Then the priests brought the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH to its place in the inner sanctuary of the house, in the Most Holy Place, underneath the wings of the cherubim.

7 For the cherubim spread out their wings over the place of the Ark, so that the cherubim overshadowed the Ark and its poles.

8 And the poles were so long that the ends of the poles were seen from the holy place before the inner sanctuary, but they could not be seen from outside. And they are there to this day.

9 There was nothing in the Ark except the two tablets of stone that Moshe put there at Khorev, where YHWH made a covenant with the people of Yisra'EL, when they came out of the land of Egypt.

10 And when the priests came out of the holy place, a cloud filled the house of YHWH,

11 So that the priests could not stand to minister because of the cloud, for the glory of YHWH filled the house of YHWH.

12 Then Shelomoh said, YHWH has said that he would dwell in thick darkness.

13 I have indeed built you an exalted house, a place for you to dwell in forever.

14 Then the king turned around and blessed all the assembly of Yisra'EL, while all the assembly of Yisra'EL stood.

15 And he said, Blessed be YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, who with his hand has fulfilled what he promised with his mouth to Dawid my father, saying,

16 Since the day that I brought my people Yisra'EL out of Egypt, I chose no city out of all the tribes of Yisra'EL in which to build a house, that my name might be there. But I chose Dawid to be over my people Yisra'EL.

17 Now it was in the heart of Dawid my father to build a house for the name of YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL.

18 But YHWH said to Dawid my father, Whereas it was in your heart to build a house for my name, you did well that it was in your heart.

19 Nevertheless, you shall not build the house, but your son who shall be born to you shall build the house for my name.

20 Now YHWH has fulfilled his promise that he made. For I have risen in the place of Dawid my father, and I sit on the throne of Yisra'EL, as YHWH promised, and I have built the house for the name of YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL.

21 And there I have provided a place for the Ark, in which is the covenant of YHWH that he made with our fathers, when he brought them out of the land of Egypt.

22 Then Shelomoh stood before the altar of YHWH in the presence of all the assembly of Yisra'EL and spread out his hands toward heaven,

23 And said, O YHWH, Creator of Yisra'EL, there is no Creator like you, in heaven above or on earth beneath, keeping covenant and showing steadfast love to your servants who walk before you with all their heart.

24 You have kept with your servant Dawid my father what you declared to him. You spoke with your mouth, and with your hand have fulfilled it this day.

25 Now therefore, O YHWH, Creator of Yisra'EL, keep for your servant Dawid my father what you have promised him, saying, You shall not lack a man to sit before me on the throne of Yisra'EL, if only your sons pay close attention to their way, to walk before me as you have walked before me.

26 Now therefore, O Creator of Yisra'EL, let your word be confirmed, which you have spoken to your servant Dawid my father.

27 But will Creator indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you — how much less this house that I have built.

28 Yet have regard to the prayer of your servant and to his plea, O YHWH my Creator, listening to the cry and to the prayer that your servant prays before you this day,

29 That your eyes may be open night and day toward this house, the place of which you have said, My name shall be there, that you may listen to the prayer that your servant offers toward this place.

30 And listen to the plea of your servant and of your people Yisra'EL, when they pray toward this place. And listen in heaven your dwelling place, and when you hear, forgive.

31 If a man sins against his neighbor and is made to take an oath and comes and swears his oath before your altar in this house,

32 Then hear in heaven and act and judge your servants, condemning the guilty by bringing his conduct on his own head, and vindicating the righteous by rewarding him according to his righteousness.

33 When your people Yisra'EL are defeated before the enemy because they have sinned against you, and if they turn again to you and acknowledge your name and pray and plead with you in this house,

34 Then hear in heaven and forgive the sin of your people Yisra'EL and bring them again to the land that you gave to their fathers.

35 When heaven is shut up and there is no rain because they have sinned against you, if they pray toward this place and acknowledge your name and turn from their sin, when you afflict them,

36 Then hear in heaven and forgive the sin of your servants, your people Yisra'EL, when you teach them the good way in which they should walk, and grant rain upon your land, which you have given to your people as an inheritance.

37 If there is famine in the land, if there is pestilence or blight or mildew or locust or caterpillar, if their enemy besieges them in the land at their gates, whatever plague, whatever sickness there is,

38 Whatever prayer, whatever plea is made by any man or by all your people Yisra'EL, each knowing the affliction of his own heart and stretching out his hands toward this house,

39 Then hear in heaven your dwelling place and forgive and act and render to each whose heart you know, according to all his ways — for you, you only, know the hearts of all the children of mankind —

40 That they may fear you all the days that they live in the land that you gave to our fathers.

41 Likewise, when a foreigner, who is not of your people Yisra'EL, comes from a far country for your name's sake

42 — For they shall hear of your great name and your mighty hand and your outstretched arm — when he comes and prays toward this house,

43 Hear in heaven your dwelling place and do according to all for which the foreigner calls to you, in order that all the peoples of the earth may know your name and fear you, as do your people Yisra'EL, and that they may know that this house that I have built is called by your name.

44 If your people go out to battle against their enemy, by whatever way you shall send them, and they pray to YHWH toward the city that you have chosen and the house that I have built for your name,

45 Then hear in heaven their prayer and their plea, and maintain their cause.

46 If they sin against you — for there is no one who does not sin — and you are angry with them and give them to an enemy, so that they are carried away captive to the land of the enemy, far off or near,

47 Yet if they turn their heart in the land to which they have been carried captive, and repent and plead with you in the land of their captors, saying, We have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly,

48 If they repent with all their heart and with all their soul in the land of their enemies, who carried them captive, and pray to you toward their land, which you gave to their fathers, the city that you have chosen, and the house that I have built for your name,

49 Then hear in heaven your dwelling place their prayer and their plea, and maintain their cause

50 And forgive your people who have sinned against you, and all their transgressions that they have committed against you, and grant them compassion in the sight of those who carried them captive, that they may have compassion on them.

51 For they are your people, and your heritage, whom you brought out of Egypt, from the midst of the iron furnace.

52 Let your eyes be open to the plea of your servant and to the plea of your people Yisra'EL, giving ear to them whenever they call to you.

53 For you separated them from among all the peoples of the earth to be your heritage, as you declared through Moshe your servant, when you brought our fathers out of Egypt, O YHWH Creator.

54 Now as Shelomoh finished offering all this prayer and plea to YHWH, he arose from before the altar of YHWH, where he had knelt with his hands spread out toward heaven.

55 And he stood and blessed all the assembly of Yisra'EL with a loud voice, saying,

56 Blessed be YHWH who has given rest to his people Yisra'EL, according to all that he promised. Not one word has failed of all his good promise, which he spoke by Moshe his servant.

57 YHWH our Creator be with us, as he was with our fathers. May he not leave us or forsake us,

58 That he may incline our hearts to him, to walk in all his ways and to keep his commandments, his statutes, and his rules, which he commanded our fathers.

59 Let these words of mine, with which I have pleaded before YHWH, be near to YHWH our Creator day and night, and may he maintain the cause of his servant and the cause of his people Yisra'EL, as each day requires,

60 That all the peoples of the earth may know that YHWH is Creator — there is no other.

61 Let your heart therefore be wholly true to YHWH our Creator, walking in his statutes and keeping his commandments, as at this day.

62 Then the king, and all Yisra'EL with him, offered sacrifice before YHWH.

63 Shelomoh offered as peace offerings to YHWH twenty-two thousand oxen and one hundred and twenty thousand sheep. So the king and all the people of Yisra'EL dedicated the house of YHWH.

64 The same day the king consecrated the middle of the court that was before the house of YHWH, for there he offered the burnt offering and the grain offering and the fat pieces of the peace offerings, because the bronze altar that was before YHWH was too small to receive the burnt offering and the grain offering and the fat pieces of the peace offerings.

65 So Shelomoh held the feast at that time, and all Yisra'EL with him, a great assembly, from Lebo-Khamath to the Brook of Egypt, before YHWH our Creator, seven days and seven days — fourteen days.

66 On the eighth day he sent the people away, and they blessed the king and went to their homes joyful and glad of heart for all the goodness that YHWH had shown to Dawid his servant and to Yisra'EL his people.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 8 is the theological centerpiece of Melakhim Aleph and one of the most sustained covenant prayers in the entire Nevi'im. Verse 27 — will Creator indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you — how much less this house — is the most theologically precise statement of divine transcendence in the dedication.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 46-51 — the prayer for restoration from exile — if they turn their heart in the land of their captors and repent with all their heart and soul and pray toward this house — hear in heaven and forgive and grant them compassion — is read as the prophetic anticipation of the Babylonian exile built into the very dedication of the temple.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 9 — there was nothing in the Ark except the two tablets of stone that Moshe put there at Khorev — is the covenant declaration of what the Ark contained at the time of the temple. The tablets of the covenant — the ten words — are the sole content of the covenant chest.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 27 — heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you — how much less this house. Shelomoh builds the greatest structure in Yisra'EL's history and in the very act of dedicating it declares its fundamental inadequacy as a container for YHWH. The covenant wisdom is extraordinary: the king who built a house for YHWH knows YHWH cannot be housed. The temple is not YHWH's residence but the place toward which YHWH's people pray — verse 29-30. The prayer is directed toward the house; the answer comes from heaven — verse 30. Verse 56 — not one word has failed of all his good promise, which he spoke by Moshe his servant. The third covenant declaration of YHWH's zero-failure record — after Yehoshua 21:45 and 23:14 — confirming the covenant testimony that spans from the wilderness to the temple.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 9 YHWH Appears to Shelomoh Again — The Covenant Warning — Khiram's Cities — Shelomoh's Labor

1 As soon as Shelomoh had finished building the house of YHWH and the king's house and all that Shelomoh desired to build,

2 YHWH appeared to Shelomoh a second time, as he had appeared to him at Giv'on.

3 And YHWH said to him, I have heard your prayer and your plea, which you have made before me. I have consecrated this house that you have built, by putting my name there forever. My eyes and my heart will be there for all time.

4 And as for you, if you will walk before me, as Dawid your father walked, with integrity of heart and uprightness, doing according to all that I have commanded you, and keeping my statutes and my rules,

5 Then I will establish your royal throne over Yisra'EL forever, as I promised Dawid your father, saying, You shall not lack a man on the throne of Yisra'EL.

6 But if you turn aside from following me, you or your children, and do not keep my commandments and my statutes that I have set before you, but go and serve other gods and worship them,

7 Then I will cut off Yisra'EL from the land that I have given them, and the house that I have consecrated for my name I will cast out of my sight, and Yisra'EL will become a proverb and a byword among all peoples.

8 And this house will become a heap of ruins. Everyone passing by it will be astonished and will hiss, and they will say, Why has YHWH done thus to this land and to this house?

9 Then they will say, Because they abandoned YHWH their Creator who brought their fathers out of the land of Egypt and laid hold on other gods and worshiped them and served them. Therefore YHWH has brought all this disaster on them.

10 At the end of twenty years, in which Shelomoh had built the two houses, the house of YHWH and the king's house,

11 And Khiram king of Tzor had supplied Shelomoh with cedar and cypress timber and gold, as much as he desired, King Shelomoh gave to Khiram twenty cities in the land of Galil.

12 But when Khiram came from Tzor to see the cities that Shelomoh had given him, they did not please him.

13 So he said, What kind of cities are these that you have given me, my brother? And he called them the land of Kavul to this day.

14 Khiram had sent to the king one hundred and twenty talents of gold.

15 And this is the account of the forced labor that King Shelomoh drafted to build the house of YHWH and his own house and the Millo and the wall of Yerushalayim and Khatzor and Megiddo and Gezer.

16 — Pharaoh king of Egypt had gone up and captured Gezer and burned it with fire, and had killed the Kena'anites who lived in the city, and had given it as dowry to his daughter, Shelomoh's wife —

17 So Shelomoh rebuilt Gezer — and Lower Beit-Kharon

18 And Ba'alat and Tamar in the wilderness, in the land of Yehudah,

19 And all the store cities that Shelomoh had, and the cities for his chariots, and the cities for his horsemen, and whatever Shelomoh desired to build in Yerushalayim, in Lebanon, and in all the land of his dominion.

20 All the people who were left of the Amorites, the Khittites, the Perizzites, the Khivvites, and the Yevusites, who were not of the people of Yisra'EL —

21 Their descendants who were left after them in the land, whom the people of Yisra'EL were unable to devote to destruction — these Shelomoh drafted to be slaves, and so they are to this day.

22 But of the people of Yisra'EL Shelomoh made no slaves. They were the soldiers, they were his officials, his commanders, his captains, his chariot commanders, and his horsemen.

23 These were the chief officers who were over Shelomoh's work: five hundred and fifty, who had charge of the people who carried on the work.

24 But Pharaoh's daughter went up from the city of Dawid to her own house that Shelomoh had built for her. Then he built the Millo.

25 Three times a year Shelomoh used to offer up burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar that he built to YHWH, making offerings on it before YHWH. So he finished the house.

26 King Shelomoh built a fleet of ships at Ezyon-Gever, which is near Elot on the shore of the Red Sea, in the land of Edom.

27 And Khiram sent with the fleet his servants, seamen who were familiar with the sea, together with the servants of Shelomoh.

28 And they went to Ofir and brought from there gold, four hundred and twenty talents, and they brought it to King Shelomoh.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 9 contains the second divine appearance to Shelomoh — mirroring the Giv'on dream of Chapter 3 — and with it the covenant warning that is the shadow over the entire Shelomoh narrative: if you turn aside, I will cut off Yisra'EL from the land and cast the house out of my sight.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verses 6-9 — YHWH's declaration of what will happen if Shelomoh and his children turn from the covenant — is read as the prophetic anticipation built into the covenant blessing of the temple dedication. The covenant warning and the covenant blessing are inseparable.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 7 — I will cut off Yisra'EL from the land — and the house that I have consecrated for my name I will cast out of my sight. The temple that has just been consecrated with YHWH's glory — verse 8:11 — is described here as capable of being rejected. The glory that filled the house can also leave it — as Yehezkel will record centuries later.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-5 — I have consecrated this house — my eyes and my heart will be there for all time — if you walk before me as Dawid walked — I will establish your throne forever. The second appearance to Shelomoh is the covenant renewal after the temple dedication. YHWH accepts the house. He commits his eyes and heart to it. And he restates the condition: if you walk before me. Verse 6-9 — but if you turn aside — I will cut off Yisra'EL from the land. The most explicit covenant warning in Melakhim Aleph is placed immediately after the temple dedication. YHWH accepts the house and in the same breath declares the conditions under which it will be destroyed. The covenant is bilateral to the end.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 10 The Queen of Sheva — She Had Heard of Shelomoh's Fame — Shelomoh's Wealth and Wisdom — Gold and Ivory

1 Now when the queen of Sheva heard of the fame of Shelomoh concerning the name of YHWH, she came to test him with hard questions.

2 She came to Yerushalayim with a very great retinue, with camels bearing spices and very much gold and precious stones. And when she came to Shelomoh, she told him all that was on her mind.

3 And Shelomoh answered all her questions — there was nothing hidden from the king that he could not explain to her.

4 And when the queen of Sheva had seen all the wisdom of Shelomoh, the house that he had built,

5 The food of his table, the seating of his officials, and the attendance of his servants, their clothing, his cupbearers, and his burnt offerings that he offered at the house of YHWH, there was no more breath in her.

6 And she said to the king, The report was true that I heard in my own land of your words and of your wisdom,

7 But I did not believe the reports until I came and my own eyes had seen it. And behold, the half was not told me. Your wisdom and prosperity surpass the report that I heard.

8 Happy are your men. Happy are your servants, who continually stand before you and hear your wisdom.

9 Blessed be YHWH your Creator, who has delighted in you and set you on the throne of Yisra'EL. Because YHWH loved Yisra'EL forever, he has made you king, that you may execute justice and righteousness.

10 Then she gave the king a hundred and twenty talents of gold, and a very great quantity of spices and precious stones. Never again came such an abundance of spices as these that the queen of Sheva gave to King Shelomoh.

11 Moreover, the fleet of Khiram, which brought gold from Ofir, brought from Ofir a very great amount of almug wood and precious stones.

12 And the king made of the almug wood supports for the house of YHWH and for the king's house, lyres also and harps for the singers. No such almug wood has come or been seen to this day.

13 And King Shelomoh gave to the queen of Sheva all that she desired, whatever she asked, besides what was given her by the bounty of King Shelomoh. So she turned and went back to her own land with her servants.

14 Now the weight of gold that came to Shelomoh in one year was six hundred and sixty-six talents of gold,

15 Besides that which came from the explorers and from the business of the merchants, and from all the kings of the west and from the governors of the land.

16 King Shelomoh made two hundred large shields of beaten gold — six hundred shekels of gold went into each shield.

17 And he made three hundred shields of beaten gold — three minas of gold went into each shield. And the king put them in the House of the Forest of Lebanon.

18 The king also made a great ivory throne and overlaid it with the finest gold.

19 The throne had six steps, and the throne had a rounded top, and on each side of the seat were armrests and two lions standing beside the armrests.

20 And twelve lions stood there, one on each end of a step on the six steps. The like of it was never made in any kingdom.

21 All King Shelomoh's drinking vessels were of gold, and all the vessels of the House of the Forest of Lebanon were of pure gold. None were of silver — silver was not considered as anything in the days of Shelomoh.

22 For the king had a fleet of ships of Tarshish at sea with the fleet of Khiram. Once every three years the fleet of ships of Tarshish used to come bringing gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.

23 Thus King Shelomoh excelled all the kings of the earth in riches and in wisdom.

24 And the whole earth sought the presence of Shelomoh to hear his wisdom, which Creator had put into his mind.

25 Every one of them brought his present, articles of silver and gold, garments, myrrh, spices, horses, and mules, so much year by year.

26 And Shelomoh gathered together chariots and horsemen. He had fourteen hundred chariots and twelve thousand horsemen, whom he stationed in the chariot cities and with the king in Yerushalayim.

27 And the king made silver as common in Yerushalayim as stone, and he made cedar as plentiful as the sycamore of the Shephelah.

28 And Shelomoh's import of horses was from Egypt and Kue, and the king's traders received them from Kue at a price.

29 A chariot could be imported from Egypt for six hundred shekels of silver and a horse for a hundred and fifty, and so through the king's traders they were exported to all the kings of the Khittites and the kings of Aram.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. The queen of Sheva narrative — verses 1-13 — is the covenant testimony of an international ruler who comes to test Shelomoh's wisdom and leaves acknowledging YHWH. Her blessing — verse 9 — blessed be YHWH your Creator who has delighted in you and set you on the throne of Yisra'EL — is the covenant declaration of a foreign queen.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full, with the Ge'ez tradition maintaining a strong connection to the queen of Sheva as Makeda of Ethiopia — the founding narrative of the Solomonic dynasty of Ethiopia. The Orit Ge'ez reads this chapter within the living memory of its own covenant royal genealogy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verses 26-29 — Shelomoh's accumulation of chariots, horses, and trading networks with Egypt — directly violates the covenant law of the king in Devarim 17:16 — he shall not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt in order to acquire many horses. The covenant shadow falls across Shelomoh's greatest prosperity.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — the queen of Sheva's blessing — because YHWH loved Yisra'EL forever, he has made you king, that you may execute justice and righteousness. The queen of a foreign nation articulates the covenant purpose of the Davidic monarchy more clearly than many within the covenant community. The king is made king not for his own glory but so that justice and righteousness may be executed in YHWH's name. Verse 23 — Shelomoh excelled all the kings of the earth in riches and in wisdom. The verse is the covenant peak of the Shelomoh narrative — and the last verse before Chapter 11 documents his covenant collapse. The wealth and wisdom are real. What comes next is the covenant failure that makes them a warning rather than a model.

SHELOMOH'S FAILURE AND THE DIVISION OF THE KINGDOM — CHAPTERS 11 THROUGH 12

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 11 Shelomoh's Foreign Wives — His Heart Turned Away — YHWH's Anger — The Kingdom Torn — Yarovam

1 Now King Shelomoh loved many foreign women, along with the daughter of Pharaoh: Moavite, Ammonite, Edomite, Tzidonite, and Khittite women,

2 From the nations concerning which YHWH had said to the people of Yisra'EL, You shall not enter into marriage with them, neither shall they with you, for surely they will turn away your heart after their gods. Shelomoh clung to these in love.

3 He had seven hundred wives who were princesses and three hundred concubines. And his wives turned away his heart.

4 For when Shelomoh was old, his wives turned away his heart after other gods, and his heart was not wholly true to YHWH his Creator, as was the heart of Dawid his father.

5 For Shelomoh went after Ashtoreth the goddess of the Tzidonians, and after Milkom the abomination of the Ammonites.

6 So Shelomoh did what was evil in the sight of YHWH and did not wholly follow YHWH, as Dawid his father had done.

7 Then Shelomoh built a high place for Kemosh the abomination of Moav, and for Molekh the abomination of the Ammonites, on the mountain east of Yerushalayim.

8 And so he did for all his foreign wives, who made offerings and sacrificed to their gods.

9 And YHWH was angry with Shelomoh, because his heart had turned away from YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, who had appeared to him twice

10 And had commanded him concerning this thing, that he should not go after other gods. But he did not keep what YHWH commanded.

11 Therefore YHWH said to Shelomoh, Since this has been your practice and you have not kept my covenant and my statutes that I have commanded you, I will surely tear the kingdom from you and will give it to your servant.

12 Yet for the sake of Dawid your father I will not do it in your days, but I will tear it out of the hand of your son.

13 However, I will not tear away all the kingdom, but I will give one tribe to your son, for the sake of Dawid my servant and for the sake of Yerushalayim, which I have chosen.

14 And YHWH raised up an adversary against Shelomoh, Hadad the Edomite. He was of the royal house in Edom.

15 For when Dawid was in Edom, and Yoav the commander of the army went up to bury the slain, he struck down every male in Edom

16 — For Yoav and all Yisra'EL remained there six months, until he had cut off every male in Edom —

17 But Hadad fled to Egypt, together with certain Edomites of his father's servants, Hadad being yet a young child.

18 They arose from Midyan and came to Paran and took men with them from Paran and came to Egypt, to Pharaoh king of Egypt, who gave him a house and assigned him an allowance of food and gave him land.

19 And Hadad found great favor in the sight of Pharaoh, so that he gave him in marriage the sister of his own wife, the sister of Takhpenes the queen.

20 And the sister of Takhpenes bore him Genuva his son, whom Takhpenes weaned in Pharaoh's house. And Genuva was in Pharaoh's house among the sons of Pharaoh.

21 But when Hadad heard in Egypt that Dawid slept with his fathers and that Yoav the commander of the army was dead, Hadad said to Pharaoh, Let me depart, that I may go to my own country.

22 But Pharaoh said to him, What have you lacked with me that you are now seeking to go to your own country? And he said, Only let me go.

23 Creator also raised up as an adversary to him Rezon the son of Elyada, who had fled from his master Hadadezer king of Tzovah.

24 And he gathered men around him and became leader of a marauding band, after the killing by Dawid. And they went to Damascus and lived there and made him king in Damascus.

25 He was an adversary of Yisra'EL all the days of Shelomoh, doing harm as Hadad did. And he loathed Yisra'EL and reigned over Aram.

26 Yarovam the son of Nevat, an Efrathite of Tzeredah, a servant of Shelomoh, whose mother's name was Tzeru'a, a widow, also lifted up his hand against the king.

27 And this was the reason why he lifted up his hand against the king. Shelomoh built the Millo and closed up the breach of the city of Dawid his father.

28 The man Yarovam was very able, and when Shelomoh saw that the young man was industrious, he gave him charge over all the forced labor of the house of Yosef.

29 And at that time, when Yarovam went out of Yerushalayim, the prophet Akhiyah the Shilonite found him on the road. Now Akhiyah had dressed himself in a new garment, and the two of them were alone in the open country.

30 Then Akhiyah laid hold of the new garment that was on him, and tore it into twelve pieces.

31 And he said to Yarovam, Take for yourself ten pieces, for thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, Behold, I am about to tear the kingdom from the hand of Shelomoh and will give you ten tribes

32 — But he shall have one tribe, for the sake of my servant Dawid and for the sake of Yerushalayim, the city that I have chosen out of all the tribes of Yisra'EL —

33 Because he has forsaken me and worshiped Ashtoreth the goddess of the Tzidonians, Kemosh the god of Moav, and Milkom the god of the Ammonites, and has not walked in my ways, doing what is right in my sight and keeping my statutes and my rules, as Dawid his father did.

34 Nevertheless, I will not take the whole kingdom out of his hand, but I will make him ruler all the days of his life, for the sake of Dawid my servant whom I chose, who kept my commandments and my statutes.

35 But I will take the kingdom out of his son's hand and will give it to you, ten tribes.

36 Yet to his son I will give one tribe, that Dawid my servant may always have a lamp before me in Yerushalayim, the city where I have chosen to put my name.

37 And I will take you, and you shall reign over all that your soul desires, and you shall be king over Yisra'EL.

38 And if you will listen to all that I command you, and will walk in my ways, and do what is right in my eyes by keeping my statutes and my commandments, as Dawid my servant did, I will be with you and will build you a sure house, as I built for Dawid, and I will give Yisra'EL to you.

39 And I will afflict the offspring of Dawid because of this, but not forever.

40 Shelomoh sought therefore to kill Yarovam. But Yarovam arose and fled into Egypt, to Shishak king of Egypt, and was in Egypt until the death of Shelomoh.

41 Now the rest of the acts of Shelomoh, and all that he did, and his wisdom, are they not written in the Book of the Acts of Shelomoh?

42 And the time that Shelomoh reigned in Yerushalayim over all Yisra'EL was forty years.

43 And Shelomoh slept with his fathers and was buried in the city of Dawid his father. And Rekhavam his son reigned in his place.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 11 is the covenant accounting of Shelomoh's failure — the man who received the greatest wisdom from YHWH, built the greatest house for YHWH's name, and offered the most theologically profound covenant prayer in the Nevi'im, ends his life worshiping the gods of his foreign wives.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full. Verse 4 — when Shelomoh was old, his wives turned away his heart after other gods, and his heart was not wholly true to YHWH, as was the heart of Dawid his father — is read as the covenant tragedy of a life: the wisdom given at the beginning, dissipated by the accumulation of covenant violations across a lifetime.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 9-10 — YHWH was angry with Shelomoh because his heart had turned away — who had appeared to him twice — and had commanded him concerning this thing. The weight of the judgment is proportional to the privilege: YHWH appeared to Shelomoh twice. Shelomoh disobeyed with full knowledge.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — his heart was not wholly true to YHWH his Creator, as was the heart of Dawid his father. The contrast between Shelomoh and Dawid is not that Dawid was morally superior — the narrative of Bathsheva and Uriyah makes that impossible. The contrast is in the fundamental orientation of the heart. Dawid's failures were departures from a covenant foundation that he returned to. Shelomoh's failures were the gradual erosion of the foundation itself — beginning with many wives, accumulating horses from Egypt, building high places for foreign gods. Each step a small violation. The accumulated effect is apostasy. Verse 36 — that Dawid my servant may always have a lamp before me in Yerushalayim. The covenant is not annulled. One tribe remains. The lamp of the Davidic covenant continues to burn even after Shelomoh's failure.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 12 Rekhavam's Folly — The Kingdom Divided — Yarovam Made King Over Yisra'EL — The Golden Calves

1 Rekhavam went to Shekhem, for all Yisra'EL had come to Shekhem to make him king.

2 And as soon as Yarovam the son of Nevat heard of it — for he was still in Egypt, where he had fled from King Shelomoh — then Yarovam returned from Egypt.

3 And they sent and called him, and Yarovam and all the assembly of Yisra'EL came and said to Rekhavam,

4 Your father made our yoke heavy. Now therefore lighten the hard service of your father and his heavy yoke on us, and we will serve you.

5 He said to them, Go away for three days, then come again to me. So the people went away.

6 Then King Rekhavam took counsel with the old men, who had stood before Shelomoh his father while he was yet alive, saying, How do you advise me to answer this people?

7 And they said to him, If you will be a servant to this people today and serve them, and speak good words to them when you answer them, then they will be your servants forever.

8 But he abandoned the counsel that the old men gave him and took counsel with the young men who had grown up with him and stood before him.

9 And he said to them, What do you advise that we answer this people who have said to me, Lighten the yoke that your father put on us?

10 And the young men who had grown up with him said to him, Thus shall you speak to this people who said to you, Your father made our yoke heavy, but you lighten it for us — thus shall you say to them, My little finger is thicker than my father's thigh.

11 And now, whereas my father laid on you a heavy yoke, I will add to your yoke. My father disciplined you with whips, but I will discipline you with scorpions.

12 So Yarovam and all the people came to Rekhavam the third day, as the king said, Come to me again the third day.

13 And the king answered the people harshly, and forsaking the counsel that the old men had given him,

14 He spoke to them according to the counsel of the young men, saying, My father made your yoke heavy, but I will add to your yoke. My father disciplined you with whips, but I will discipline you with scorpions.

15 So the king did not listen to the people, for it was a turn of affairs brought about by YHWH that he might fulfill his word, which YHWH spoke by Akhiyah the Shilonite to Yarovam the son of Nevat.

16 And when all Yisra'EL saw that the king did not listen to them, the people answered the king, What portion do we have in Dawid? We have no inheritance in the son of Yishai. To your tents, O Yisra'EL. Look now to your own house, O Dawid. So Yisra'EL went to their tents.

17 But Rekhavam reigned over the people of Yisra'EL who lived in the cities of Yehudah.

18 Then King Rekhavam sent Adoram, who was taskmaster over the forced labor, and all Yisra'EL stoned him to death with stones. And King Rekhavam hurried to mount his chariot to flee to Yerushalayim.

19 So Yisra'EL has been in rebellion against the house of Dawid to this day.

20 And when all Yisra'EL heard that Yarovam had returned, they sent and called him to the assembly and made him king over all Yisra'EL. There was none that followed the house of Dawid but the tribe of Yehudah only.

21 When Rekhavam came to Yerushalayim, he assembled all the house of Yehudah and the tribe of Binyamin, one hundred and eighty thousand chosen warriors, to fight against the house of Yisra'EL, to restore the kingdom to Rekhavam the son of Shelomoh.

22 But the word of Creator came to Shemayah the man of Creator:

23 Say to Rekhavam the son of Shelomoh, king of Yehudah, and to all the house of Yehudah and Binyamin, and to the rest of the people,

24 Thus says YHWH, You shall not go up or fight against your relatives the people of Yisra'EL. Every man return to his home, for this thing is from me. So they listened to the word of YHWH and went home again, according to the word of YHWH.

25 Then Yarovam built Shekhem in the hill country of Efrayim and lived there. And he went out from there and built Penu'el.

26 And Yarovam said in his heart, Now the kingdom will turn back to the house of Dawid.

27 If this people go up to offer sacrifices in the house of YHWH at Yerushalayim, then the heart of this people will turn again to their lord, to Rekhavam king of Yehudah, and they will kill me and return to Rekhavam king of Yehudah.

28 So the king took counsel and made two calves of gold. And he said to the people, You have gone up to Yerushalayim long enough. Behold your gods, O Yisra'EL, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt.

29 And he set one in Beit-El, and the other he put in Dan.

30 Then this thing became a sin, for the people went as far as Dan to be before one.

31 He also made temples on high places and appointed priests from among all the people, who were not of the Levites.

32 And Yarovam appointed a feast on the fifteenth day of the eighth month like the feast that was in Yehudah, and he offered sacrifices on the altar. So he did in Beit-El, sacrificing to the calves that he made. And he placed in Beit-El the priests of the high places that he had made.

33 He went up to the altar that he had made in Beit-El on the fifteenth day in the eighth month, in the month that he had devised from his own heart. And he instituted a feast for the people of Yisra'EL and went up to the altar to make offerings.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. The division of the kingdom is the covenant catastrophe prophesied by Akhiyah in Chapter 11 and fulfilled in Chapter 12. Rekhavam's rejection of the wise counsel of the elders — and acceptance of the reckless counsel of his young companions — is the political folly that fulfills the covenant prophecy.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full. Verse 15 — the king did not listen to the people, for it was a turn of affairs brought about by YHWH to fulfill his word — is read as the covenant declaration of divine sovereignty in political folly: YHWH uses Rekhavam's arrogance to accomplish the covenant judgment pronounced against Shelomoh's house.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 28-29 — Yarovam's golden calves — you have gone up to Yerushalayim long enough, behold your gods O Yisra'EL who brought you up out of Egypt — directly echoes the golden calf of Shemot 32:4 and 32:8. The same words, the same formula, the same covenant violation repeated at Beit-El and Dan.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 28 — behold your gods, O Yisra'EL, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt. The exact words of Aharon at Sinai in Shemot 32:4. Yarovam repeats the greatest covenant betrayal in Yisra'EL's history, now institutionalizing it as the state religion of the northern kingdom. His political motivation — verse 26-27 — is transparent: if the people go to Yerushalayim to worship, they will return to the house of Dawid. The calves are political instruments masquerading as covenant worship. Verse 33 — a feast that he had devised from his own heart. The covenant alternative to YHWH's commanded calendar — a feast invented by the king's heart to replace the feast commanded by YHWH. The formula that will condemn every northern king: the sin of Yarovam the son of Nevat, which he made Yisra'EL to sin.

END OF PART TWO — CHAPTERS 7 THROUGH 12
